summary: Gruber argues that TV channels should become apps, just as Newsstand turned magazines and newspapers into apps on iOS. Inspired by Jason Kottke's observation connecting Newsstand's success with Bloomberg TV+, Gruber theorizes this is what Steve Jobs meant by having 'cracked' television. He notes that Apple chose apps as the unit of distribution for print media rather than building a single reader app, giving publishers more control and creativity. He extends this logic to television: networks like ESPN and HBO already have iOS apps, and making TV channels into interactive apps on a TV-sized display would benefit viewers, networks, and advertisers alike. This would form the basis of a future Apple TV product.
key_points: Apple's Newsstand strategy made the app — not the issue or article — the unit of distribution for print media, giving publishers more control
TV networks are already building iOS apps (HBO Go, ESPN, Bloomberg TV+) that combine video with interactive features and subscriber verification
An Apple television set could use apps as channels, enabling interactive features like on-screen stats during a baseball game
This app-as-channel model would eliminate the need for Nielsen-style ratings by providing exact viewership analytics
Gruber believes this vision is what Jobs meant when he said he had 'finally cracked' the TV problem
money_quotes: The app is the unit of distribution for newspapers and magazines, not the 'issue' or the 'article'.

This puts more work on the publishers' shoulders — they need to design, create, and maintain software, not merely publish content — but it gives them more control over the reader experience and more potential for creativity and differentiation.
Why not the same thing for TV channels?
Imagine watching a baseball game on a TV where ESPN is a smart app, not a dumb channel.
It'd be better for both viewers and the networks if a TV 'channel' were an interactive app rather than a mere single stream of video.
Collect them in a Newsstand-like folder on iPhones and iPads, and make them the 'home screen' of a future Apple TV.
There was a palpable, 'Damn, I was expecting Steve Jobs to save our industry today' reaction amongst the media contingent.
Apps allow for fairly exact viewership statistics. There'd be no need for Nielsen-style statistical polling if exact analytics are available.
key_insight: The future of television lies in replacing dumb channels with smart, interactive apps — the same transformation Apple's Newsstand brought to print media.
